title: Cordoning and Uncordoning a Node
description:
## Cordoning and Uncordoning a Node

`kubectl cordon` marks a node as **unschedulable** — the node continues running existing workloads but the scheduler will not place new pods on it. This is useful before maintenance windows.

`kubectl uncordon` reverses the cordon, making the node schedulable again.

**Your task:**

1. Find the name of the cluster node using `kubectl get nodes`
2. **Cordon** the node
3. Verify the node shows `SchedulingDisabled` in its status
4. **Uncordon** the node to restore it to normal

```bash
# Check node status:
kubectl get nodes

# Cordon:
kubectl cordon <node-name>

# Uncordon:
kubectl uncordon <node-name>
```

hints.title: Find the node name
hints.body: In this single-node cluster, there is exactly one node. Use kubectl get nodes to find its name.

hints.title: Cordon and uncordon using a variable
hints.body: Store the node name in a variable for convenience, then cordon and uncordon it.
hints.command: NODE=$(kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') && kubectl cordon $NODE && kubectl uncordon $NODE

teardown_commands.command: kubectl uncordon $(kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') 2>/dev/null || true
